did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an Artificial Neural Network (ANN), specifically a two-layer feedforward neural network with back-propagation training, to classify non-dermoscopic skin images as malignant melanoma or benign moles based on extracted color features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the proposal of a new set of 10 color features for melanoma diagnosis, the development of a classification system using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN), and the evaluation of this system on a specific dataset of 175 non-dermoscopic skin images. It includes sections on methodology, experiments, and quantitative results comparing the proposed method to state-of-the-art algorithms, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: A Set of Descriptors for Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Non-Dermoscopic Color Images
journal: IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP)
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-aided diagnosis system for melanoma using non-dermoscopic color images captured by conventional cameras. The authors introduce a set of ten color features, including color variation, spatial distribution, and intensity, extracted via Fuzzy C-Means clustering and histogram analysis. These features are classified using a two-layer feedforward artificial neural network trained with back-propagation. The system was evaluated on a dataset of 175 images (70 melanoma, 105 benign) from the University Medical Center Groningen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms like MED-NODe and SpotMole in sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis; 70 melanoma images were used in the dataset from the University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG).
The comparator class for benign conditions; 105 (or 100 in Table 1 description) benign images were used in the dataset from the University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Malignant Melanoma vs. Benign Mole)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to classify non-dermoscopic skin images into two discrete classes: malignant melanoma or benign moles. The system outputs a diagnostic label based on extracted color features, evaluated using classification metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy.

datasets_used.name: University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG) Digital Image Archive
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of non-dermoscopic skin images captured by digital cameras, sourced from the Digital Image Archive of the Department of Dermatology of the University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG).

use.training: 125 images (50 melanoma, 75 benign) were used for training the Artificial Neural Network.
use.testing: 50 images (20 melanoma, 30 benign) were used for testing the classification system.
use.other: The dataset was also used for fair comparison with state-of-the-art methods MED-NODe and SpotMole.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure results are independent from the specific selection of images for training and testing; the mean of the obtained results was reported.
To divide the dataset into training (125 images) and testing (50 images) sets.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of classification metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Accuracy)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively evaluate and compare the proposed method against state-of-the-art algorithms (MED-NODe and SpotMole).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Accuracy) and compared to state-of-the-art methods, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

UMCG (University Medical Center Groninge.dataset_description: 175 non-dermoscopic skin images (70 melanoma, 105 benign) from the Digital Image Archive of the Department of Dermatology of the University Medical Center Groningen.
UMCG (University Medical Center Groninge.evaluation_method: Classification process repeated 1000 times with random splits; mean results reported.
UMCG (University Medical Center Groninge.notes: The proposed method outperformed MED-NODe and SpotMole in terms of sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy. Specifically, it achieved higher sensitivity than MED-NODe and higher specificity than SpotMole. The proposed method used only color features, while SpotMole used all ABCD descriptors.

architecture_used.input_size: 10-dimensional feature vector (extracted from non-dermoscopic color images)

architecture_details.layers: Two layers (one hidden layer, one output layer)

training_details.evaluation_method: Classification process repeated 1000 times with random splits; mean results reported

preprocessing_techniques: Illumination correction to eliminate rapid changes in saturation and value channels
Edge-preserving smoothing filters to remove noisy artifacts
Smoothing out sharp gradient values in color channels
Segmentation in HSV color space using K-means clustering (treing V dimension as an angle) to separate lesion from healthy skin
Morphological operations including closing, opening, and hole filling to improve segmentation mask quality
Selection of the biggest connected component in the segmentation map as the lesion region mask
Gaussian filtering (sigma=15) to reduce noisy values prior to Fuzzy C-Means clustering
conclusions: The proposed set of 10 color features, derived from color variation, spatial distribution, and intensity characteristics of non-dermoscopic skin images, is effective for distinguishing between malignant melanoma and benign moles.

The computer-aided diagnosis system utilizing these color features and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) outperforms state-of-the-art methods MED-NODe and SpotMole in terms of sensitivity, specificity, positive predicted value (PPV), negative predicted value (NPV), and accuracy.

The proposed method achieves superior performance compared to SpotMole despite using only color features, whereas SpotMole utilizes all ABCD descriptors, indicating the discriminative power of the proposed color-based feature set.

The system demonstrates that conventional user-grade camera images, which are more broadly available than dermoscopic images, can be effectively utilized for melanoma diagnosis when combined with appropriate preprocessing and feature extraction techniques.

The study acknowledges that non-dermoscopic images typically contain less detailed information and are subject to illumination and noise effects compared to dermoscopic images, necessitating robust preprocessing steps such as illumination correction and segmentation.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (175 images total: 70 melanoma, 105 benign), which may affect the generalizability of the results.
The dataset is sourced from a single institution (University Medical Center Groningen), introducing potential population and dataset bias.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal UMCG dataset.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed method and state-of-the-art algorithms (MED-NODe, SpotMole).
The proposed method uses only color features, whereas the comparator SpotMole uses all ABCD descriptors; however, the proposed method still outperformed SpotMole in four of five metrics.

The system relies on non-dermoscopic images captured by conventional user-grade cameras, which inherently contain less detailed information and are subject to illumination and noise effects compared to dermoscopic images.

The classification process was repeated 1000 times with random splits to mitigate bias from specific image selection, but the specific random seed or fixed split protocol is not detailed beyond the 3:1 train-test ratio.
The neural network architecture (hidden layer size of 15 neurons) was determined via trial and error, which is a subjective hyperparameter selection method.
